narrator_text:
The descent picks its own line — south of the bell-farmstead by two ridges, into a stand of pine that hasn't been logged in fifty years, where the canopy is thick enough to swallow a wing-spread on the first downstroke and a clearing exists at the base of a granite knuckle wide enough for an adult black dragon to set down without snapping anything that can't be replaced. Shen lands. The dragon doesn't fold so much as *unmake itself* — wings retract, scales drop their carbon-thickness back into the chest-glyph, neck shortens, jaw shortens, the body that just lifted a horseman from a saddle re-fits itself into the shape of a barefoot man in monk-pants. The dragon-tattoo on his chest pulls all the green-fire back into itself and burns there for a moment, dim and steady. *(One level of exhaustion lands on him as the form dismisses. He'll feel it in the next physical roll.)*

He walks. South. Parallel to the road but a half-mile west of it, fern-deep, picking the line a deer would pick. The wind has shifted out of the south now and brings him sounds that didn't reach the road: a creek somewhere left, a hawk that isn't his hawk, woodpigeons. After two hours the sun is angling — call it Day 3 hour 17, late afternoon turning evening — and the pine smell is thicker, older, the kind of forest that knows its own age. Three more miles south, the trees thin into a goat-track running east-to-west across his line. He can hear voices on it before he sees them.

"—not the bells from this morning, I'm telling you, *those* were the dragon bells. This is the bone-ringing pattern. The bone pattern is for *kingdom-business* coming through, riders, official —" "Aye and what difference does it make to me? My uncle rang the dragon bells when he saw a *kestrel* once. Half this valley couldn't tell a banquet from a funeral —" "Lower your voice. *Lower.* The road's right there." A pause. "...Did you hear something?" Two human silhouettes — shepherds, by the staffs and the dogs — frozen on the track twenty paces ahead of him, looking the wrong direction (north, toward the road, where they think the sound is coming from). They have not seen him.
options: Hold absolutely still in the fern-cover and let them pass; they're looking the wrong way and will move on if you don't move.
Step into the goat-track in plain view and let them see a barefoot man — see how they react when the snow-pale stranger is the one walking up.
Detour a half-mile west into the deeper forest, bypass them entirely, accept the slower pace, stay invisible.
player_action: go back to human form and continue walking to my location.
